figures.gloss: A Jeolla-province healer and teacher (1871-1909) whom the Jeungsanist religions worship as the Supreme God of the Ninth Heaven (Gucheon Sangje) descended into human flesh. After a forty-nine-day practice on Mount Moaksan in 1901 he declared his heavenly authority and spent his remaining eight years not preaching a path but, as God, ritually repairing the world itself.
figures.facet: The rare case of a tradition whose founder is not the messenger of the high god but the high god — revelation collapses into incarnation, so his biography is read as cosmic administration rather than prophecy.
figures.provenance: Jeon-gyeong (Daesoon Jinrihoe canonical scripture, 1974); Lee Kang-o (1967) 'Chungsan-gyo', trans. Richard Rutt; Massimo Introvigne (2017), World Religions and Spirituality Project entry on Daesoon Jinrihoe.

figures.gloss: The nine years of ritual works (1901-1909) by which Kang, as incarnate Sangje, is held to have re-programmed the Degree Number (dosu) of the cosmos — correcting the mutual-conflict order of the Former World and fixing the schedule of the coming Later World. The works were performed with small gestures, written charms, burned papers, and assigned roles for disciples, each act binding at cosmic scale.
figures.facet: Salvation executed as completed divine engineering: the decisive cosmic intervention lies in the past, already done, and history since is the working-out of a ritually amended program.

figures.gloss: The central Jeungsanist tenet: the Former World ran on sanggeuk, mutual overcoming, and its casualties — oppressed people, slighted spirits, even wronged beings of nature — accumulated won, grievance, that clogs the cosmos. The Later World can only run on sangsaeng, mutual life-giving, once every grievance is untied.
figures.facet: It makes theodicy procedural: evil is not rebellion or illusion but a backlog of unresolved historical injury, and the divine work is literally case-work through that backlog.
figures.provenance: Jeon-gyeong; Daesoon Jinrihoe doctrinal statements (Tenets); Introvigne (2017).

figures.gloss: The epochal turn from the Former World (seoncheon) of conflict to the Later World (hucheon) of harmony — an earthly paradise in which humans attain god-like stature and the divine and human orders are reconciled. In Jeungsanism the Opening is guaranteed, because Sangje already reset the cosmic program in the cheonji-gongsa.
figures.facet: Apocalypse with the terror amputated: because the decisive battle was fought ritually by God in 1901-09, the end-time becomes a scheduled maturation rather than a war to be won.
figures.provenance: Jeon-gyeong; shared gaebyeok vocabulary of Korea's founder religions documented in Don Baker (2008) 'Korean Spirituality'.

figures.gloss: The incantation Kang taught his followers as the essential practice for the transition to the Later World — chanted rhythmically to invoke the salvific energy of heaven, heal illness, and carry the practitioner through the coming Opening. It remains the core daily practice across Jeungsanist branches.
figures.facet: The whole soteriology is compressed into a portable act of sound: where the founder's cosmic works are finished and unrepeatable, the mantra is the one lever left in the believer's own mouth.
figures.provenance: Jeon-gyeong; Jeungsando 'Dojeon' (1992); the mantra's pre-Kang origin is contested and left unattributed here.

figures.gloss: The lineage-founder (1895-1958) whom Daesoon Jinrihoe holds to have received Kang Jeungsan's religious authority by direct revelation in 1917 — the two never met in life. He organized the scattered post-Kang following into ordered bodies (Mugeukdo 1925, Taegeukdo 1950), from which Park Wudang established Daesoon Jinrihoe in 1969.
figures.facet: Succession without contact: authority passes by revelation across a founder's death, and it is the organizer of that claim — not the god-man himself — who turns completed cosmic works into a durable institution.
figures.provenance: Daesoon Jinrihoe orthodoxy as recorded in the Jeon-gyeong and institutional histories; Introvigne (2017); the 1917 revelation is the tradition's own claim, accepted by Daesoon Jinrihoe and disputed by rival branches.

edges.note: The same historical person: Kang Il-sun, sketched in the Korean-NRM cluster shard, rendered here in his own tradition — identity, not analogy.
The identical doctrine carried in both shards — the Korean-NRM cluster holds the summary, this shard the tradition it belongs to.
The same Great Opening concept moving through Korea's founder-religion wave from Donghak into Jeungsanism; shared term and shared expectation, though Kang's sources also include Jeong Yeok cosmology.

Attested succession claim: Jeungsanist doctrine holds Sangje first commissioned Choe Je-u, and descended in person as Kang when Donghak failed — Kang's ministry began in Gobu, the very district where the 1894 Donghak revolution broke out.

Korea's high Lord of Heaven, who in the old myth delegates and remains aloft, is here declared to have come down himself — the withdrawn sky-father inverted into full incarnation; the Sangje-Hwanin/Hanunim mapping is structural, not the tradition's own wording.

Kang's reordering works speak the idiom of the Korean shamanic gut — paper charms, burned writings, seated and voiced spirits, the untying of the aggrieved dead — scaled from a household ritual to the cosmos; the kinship is documented ethnographically, not a proven borrowing.
Twin central incantations of the same Korean wave: a short, rhythmically chanted formula as the lay believer's whole portable practice — Donghak's 21-character sicheonju and Jeungsanism's Taeeulju.
Intra-tradition: the incarnate god and the nine years of ritual works that are the entire content of his earthly mission.
Intra-tradition: the Later World cannot open until the backlog of grievances is resolved — the principle is the precondition of the epoch.
Intra-tradition: the god-man and the never-met successor who received his authority by revelation in 1917 — the claim on which Daesoon Jinrihoe's legitimacy rests.
The same gaebyeok expectation of Korea's founder-religion wave, attested in Won Buddhism's own founding motto — though Won reassigns the Opening from divine works to human self-cultivation.

Era-kin founders of the same Jeolla-province religious ferment a decade apart — but inverted in self-claim: Kang declared himself the supreme god incarnate, Sotaesan claimed only an awakened human's authority.

dossier.essence: Jeungsanism is the family of Korean new religions that worship Kang Jeungsan (Kang Il-sun, 1871-1909), a Jeolla-province healer and teacher, as the Supreme God of the Ninth Heaven (Gucheon Sangje) incarnate in human flesh. The movement fragmented into dozens of orders after his death — Bocheongyo was colonial Korea's largest new religion in the 1920s — and its dominant living branch is Daesoon Jinrihoe (established 1969), one of the largest indigenous religions in South Korea, with Jeungsando as a smaller international sibling.
dossier.cosmos: The Former World (seoncheon) ran on sanggeuk, mutual overcoming, and its casualties — oppressed people, slighted spirits, wronged beings of nature — accumulated won, grievance, that clogs both the human and divine orders. Jeungsanists hold that Kang, as incarnate Sangje, spent 1901-1909 performing the cheonji-gongsa, the Reordering Works of Heaven and Earth, ritually re-programming the cosmic schedule so that the Great Opening of the Later World (hucheon gaebyeok) — an earthly paradise running on sangsaeng, mutual life-giving, in which humans attain god-like stature — is already guaranteed. The dead persist as spirits entangled in the same grievance-resolution process that history is working out.
dossier.practice: The core practice across branches is incantation, above all the Taeeul mantra Kang taught as the essential preparation for the coming Opening; Daesoon Jinrihoe adds scheduled devotional training sessions (gongbu) maintained in unbroken rotation at its Yeoju headquarters, along with prayer offerings and seasonal rituals. The governing ethic is haewon sangsaeng — resolving grievances for mutual beneficence, concretely framed as never creating new grievances in others — and the largest branch operates universities, a major hospital network, and extensive charity as doctrinal practice. Authority in Daesoon Jinrihoe descends from Kang through Jo Jeongsan, held to have received Kang's authority by revelation in 1917 without the two ever meeting, and then Park Wudang.
dossier.canon: Each branch holds its own scripture recording Kang's words and works: Daesoon Jinrihoe's Jeon-gyeong (1974; English translation 'The Canonical Scripture', 2020) and Jeungsando's Dojeon (1992) are the principal compilations.
dossier.reflects: Theodicy made procedural: evil as a historical backlog of unresolved grievances, and salvation as completed divine case-work — the decisive cosmic intervention placed in the recent past, leaving believers to live out a repair already filed.
sources: Massimo Introvigne, 'Daesoon Jinrihoe' (World Religions and Spirituality Project profile, 2017)
Lee Kang-o (trans. Richard Rutt), 'Chungsan-gyo: Its History, Doctrine and Ritual Practice', Transactions of the Royal Asiatic Society Korea Branch 43 (1967)

links.cite: Massimo Introvigne, 'Daesoon Jinrihoe' (World Religions and Spirituality Project profile, 2017)
